summary: The plan is well-aligned with the PRD and accurately reflects both post-PRD user decisions (new GitHub Actions workflow; Redoc offline single-file renderer). The 116-route count, the server-only constraint, the implicit-200 design, and the version pins are all verified correct against the codebase. The most serious gap is a class of status codes returned by SHARED HELPERS rather than by handler-local `status:` literals: the status-code extractor reads handler source text only, so a 404 produced by `gateFeature(...)` (confirmed in src/lib/features.ts:53) is invisible to it. This both understates the documented status surface for ~several gated routes AND means the coverage gate will never demand documenting that 404 — a silent honesty hole in the very gate the project sells as its drift guarantee. Two smaller inconsistencies (a self-contradicting comment about members/export, and the PRD's Scalar references not all reconciled to Redoc) are should_fix/suggestion. Net: sound plan, one must_fix on helper-returned statuses.

findings.title: Status extractor misses status codes returned by shared helpers (gateFeature 404, requirePermission 403)
findings.detail: Invariant #2 and the Task-3 extractor design only scan a route.ts's own source for `status: NNN` literals plus implicit-200. But several handlers delegate error responses to helpers that the extractor never reads. Confirmed: `gateFeature(orgId, feature)` returns `NextResponse.json({ error: 'Not found' }, { status: 404 })` (src/lib/features.ts:53), and `src/app/api/members/export/route.ts:38-39` returns that result directly via `if (gated) return gated;` — so this route can emit a 404 that appears in NO `status:` literal in members/export/route.ts. Task 7 explicitly flags `gateFeature('MEMBERSHIP_PLATFORM')` routes but assumes the gate response is locally visible. Because the coverage gate (coverage.test.ts test b) is fed by the SAME extractStatuses(), the gate will neither demand nor detect this 404. The plan's headline guarantee ('every status code each handler can return is documented') is therefore false for helper-returned statuses, and Task 2's DoD for members/export ('statuses 200/401/403') already omits the 404 the handler can actually return.
findings.location: Plan: Context for Implementer invariant #2 (line 37); Task 3 extractStatuses design (line 175); Task 7 (line 249); Task 2 DoD (line 159). Code: src/lib/features.ts:51-54; src/app/api/members/export/route.ts:38-39.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) extend extractStatuses to follow a small allowlist of status-returning helpers (gateFeature->404, requirePermission->403, and any others found in fan-out) so the gate asserts them, or (b) explicitly document in invariant #2 that helper-returned statuses are a known extractor blind spot, require the author to add them by hand per route, and add 404 to the members/export DoD and any gateFeature route. Option (a) is the only one that keeps the CI gate honest; (b) at minimum stops the plan from overclaiming. Audit all gateFeature/requirePermission/auth-helper call sites during the relevant fan-out tasks.

findings.title: Self-contradicting characterisation of members/export status (implicit vs explicit 200)
findings.detail: Task 3's route-surface.test.ts assertion describes `members/export` as '200 implicit-via-explicit' (line 176) and the risk table says 'members/export = 200 implicit-via-explicit' (line 58). The handler actually returns `new NextResponse(csv, { status: 200, ... })` (src/app/api/members/export/route.ts:83-84) — an EXPLICIT `status: 200` literal, not implicit. The phrase 'implicit-via-explicit' is self-contradicting and will confuse the implementer writing the extractor unit test: if the test asserts that the 200 here comes from the implicit-200 branch, it will be asserting the wrong code path and could mask a real extractor bug.
findings.recommendation: Correct the test description to state members/export returns an EXPLICIT `status: 200`. Use a genuinely implicit-200 route (e.g. `GET /api/animals` at src/app/api/animals/route.ts:30,49,61, all bare `NextResponse.json(animals)`) as the canonical implicit-200 fixture so the extractor's implicit branch is actually exercised by a test.

findings.title: PRD-to-plan renderer reconciliation is incomplete; PRD still says Scalar in multiple places
findings.detail: The post-PRD decision is Redoc offline single-file, and the plan correctly uses `@redocly/cli build-docs` throughout. But the PRD (the source-of-truth artifact the plan derives from) still names Scalar as the render target in In-Scope (line 42, 49), Flow 1 (line 24), Technical Context output paths (line 79), and Key Decisions (line 91 lists Scalar as the choice with Redoc as 'fallback'). A future reader diffing plan against PRD will see an apparent scope deviation. The plan's TS-001 also asserts 'Redoc reference UI' (line 80) against a PRD that says Scalar — the verification step and its source requirement disagree on the expected UI.
findings.location: Plan: Approach line 30, Task 1 line 135, TS-001 line 80. PRD: lines 24, 42, 49, 79, 91.
findings.recommendation: Add one line to the plan's Context or Approach explicitly recording that the user overrode the PRD's Scalar default in favour of the PRD's pre-authorized Redoc fallback (the PRD already sanctions this at line 91), so the deviation is documented rather than implicit. No PRD edit required, but the plan should name the override so it doesn't read as drift.

findings.title: redocly build-docs offline self-containment (zero-network render) is an untested assumption gating TS-001
findings.detail: The headline deliverable and TS-001 Step 1 require `docs/api/index.html` to render with the network disabled ('no network requests'). The plan asserts `@redocly/cli build-docs` produces a self-contained single file, but this is not verified anywhere in the codebase (the repo has no @redocly/cli today) and depends on the exact build-docs flags/version behaviour. Some Redoc/redocly build outputs still reference a CDN-hosted Redoc bundle or web fonts unless explicitly inlined. If the output pulls the Redoc JS or fonts from a CDN, TS-001 Step 1 fails despite tests passing. This is the single offline-correctness claim the whole renderer choice rests on.
findings.location: Plan: Goal Verification truth 3 (line 69), TS-001 step 1 (line 80), Task 1 docs:api:html (line 135). No codebase evidence (package has no @redocly/cli).
findings.recommendation: In Task 1 DoD, add an explicit offline assertion: after `npm run docs:api:html`, grep the generated index.html for `http://`/`https://` references to external scripts/fonts and fail if any load-bearing asset is remote (allow only data: URIs / inlined content). Confirm the @redocly/cli@2.33.2 build-docs flag that inlines assets, or document the fallback. Treat 'renders offline' as a Task-1 gate, not only a Task-11 browser check.

findings.title: tsconfig.scripts.json precedent suggests server-only isolation may need explicit guarding
findings.detail: The plan (Task 1, line 134) states generate-openapi.ts can run via plain `tsx` with 'no --tsconfig tsconfig.scripts.json needed since nothing pulls in server-only'. This is plausible and the server-only constraint is verified (rbac.ts:1 `'server-only';`, clerk-server.ts:1 `import 'server-only';`). However, package.json shows every other server-touching script (charge-due, membership-reminders, refresh-tokens at lines 31-33) deliberately uses `tsx --tsconfig tsconfig.scripts.json`. That precedent exists precisely because plain tsx + server-only crashes. The plan's correctness here hinges entirely on src/lib/openapi/ never transitively importing a server-only module — a discipline that one careless `import` in a fan-out task could violate, and the failure mode (crash under npm ci in CI) is exactly invariant #1's risk.
findings.location: Plan: Task 1 line 134, invariant #1 line 36. Code: package.json:31-33 (tsconfig.scripts.json precedent), src/lib/rbac.ts:1, src/lib/clerk-server.ts:1.
findings.recommendation: Add a cheap structural guard to the Task-3 extractor test suite or a lint step: assert that no file under src/lib/openapi/ (and scripts/generate-openapi.ts) contains an import of `@/lib/prisma`, `@/lib/clerk-server`, `@/lib/rbac`, or a `route` handler. This makes invariant #1 mechanically enforced rather than reviewer-dependent, and catches the crash before CI.

findings.title: Fan-out batch route counts are large; verify the per-task DoD actually proves completeness, not just presence
findings.detail: Tasks 4-10 document 18, 14, 20, 16, 14, 7, ~22 routes respectively. Their Verify commands mostly run `npm run docs:api && npx vitest run src/lib/openapi/coverage.test.ts` and rely on the global coverage gate going green. That is sound for route+method coverage, but the per-task DoD ('exact status codes', 'correct min role/permission', 'response schema') is NOT machine-verified by coverage.test.ts (which only asserts route/method presence and status-code presence, per line 177) — min-role/permission and response-body fidelity remain reviewer-only. A task can show 'coverage green' while a response schema is wrong. This is the plan's stated honest boundary, but the per-task DoDs read as if Verify proves the schema correct.
findings.recommendation: Reword the fan-out DoDs to distinguish CI-asserted criteria (route+method+status presence) from reviewer-asserted criteria (role/permission accuracy, body fidelity), so the implementer and /spec verify step don't treat a green coverage run as proof of schema correctness. No new tooling required.

verified_ok: Route count: 116 route.ts files under src/app/api/ confirmed (Glob enumeration totals 116 across all domains; matches the plan and PRD claim).

server-only constraint: src/lib/rbac.ts:1 is `'server-only';` and src/lib/clerk-server.ts:1 is `import 'server-only';` — the plan's assertion that handlers transitively import server-only via these modules is correct, justifying the no-handler-import generation graph (invariant #1).

Implicit-200 reality: GET /api/animals returns bare `NextResponse.json(animals)` at src/app/api/animals/route.ts:30, 49, 61 with no `status:` — confirming most 200s are implicit and the extractor must default no-status returns to 200.

animals/route.ts status surface: handler returns 401 (line 12), 400 (18,65), 403 (19,64,85), 201 (104,109), 422 (115), 500 (67,122) plus implicit 200 — matches the plan's claimed {200,201,400,401,403,422,500} fixture for the extractor test.

Export style: src/app/api/animals/route.ts uses `export async function GET`/`export async function POST` (lines 10,71) and members/export uses `export async function GET` (line 33), matching the plan's export-style assumption (the extractor also matching `export const GET =` is reasonable defensive coverage).

Uniform { error: string } envelope: confirmed in animals/route.ts (every error path uses `{ error: ... }`) and members/export/route.ts (lines 36,43), and gateFeature returns `{ error: 'Not found' }` (features.ts:53) — the dominant-envelope assumption holds.

Version pins: project uses zod ^3.25.76 (package.json:114), so zod-openapi v4 (zod-3 compatible) is the correct choice and v5 (zod-4 .meta()) would break; the plan's 'do NOT bump zod, do NOT use v5' guidance is sound. tsx ^4.22.4 is present (package.json:113) so the tsx-based generate script has its runtime.
No existing .github/workflows/: the new api-docs.yml is genuinely the repo's first workflow (no workflows dir present), matching post-PRD decision (1).
package-lock.json committed assumption / npm package manager: package.json + npm lockfile usage is consistent; npm ci in CI is valid (lockfile-based scripts like postinstall/build present).

members/export CSV response: handler returns `new NextResponse(csv, { status: 200, headers: { 'Content-Type': 'text/csv...', 'Content-Disposition': ... } })` (lines 83-89) — matches the plan's text/csv + Content-Disposition documentation requirement.
